title: Introduction to Industrial Radiographic Testing
narrationScript: On the evening of November eighth, 1895, a German physicist named Wilhelm Roentgen was working alone with a cathode ray tube in his laboratory in Wurzburg. The tube was wrapped in black cardboard, the room was dark, and yet a coated screen across the bench began to glow every time he energized the tube. Something invisible was passing through the cardboard and lighting up that screen. Roentgen spent the next weeks testing everything he could put in the path of the rays. Wood, books, rubber, metal. He found that the new rays passed easily through light materials and were stopped by dense ones. Because he did not know what they were, he called them X rays, X for unknown. Within weeks he had recorded an image of the bones in his wife's hand, and the world changed. Medicine adopted the new rays almost overnight, and engineers were not far behind. In 1901 Roentgen received the first Nobel Prize in physics for the discovery. The year after Roentgen's discovery, in 1896, the French physicist Henri Becquerel found that uranium salts fogged photographic plates straight through their light tight wrapping, with no tube and no electricity at all. Certain heavy elements emit penetrating radiation on their own, continuously, with nothing switched on. Marie and Pierre Curie chased that discovery, and by 1898 they had discovered two new radioactive elements, polonium and radium. Marie Curie gave the phenomenon its name, radioactivity. Those two discoveries, man made X rays and naturally radioactive materials, are still the two halves of our trade today. Industry put the rays to work within a generation. In the early nineteen twenties the United States Army set up the first industrial radiographic laboratory in the country, using X rays to examine castings. By the early nineteen thirties, pressure vessel codes began to require radiography of critical welds, because vessels that had only been inspected on the surface kept failing in service. The Second World War expanded the method enormously, and after the war nuclear reactors gave us man made isotopes such as iridium one ninety two and cobalt sixty, which replaced radium and made portable gamma radiography practical. So what exactly is industrial radiographic testing. It is a nondestructive testing method that passes penetrating ionizing radiation through a part and records what comes out the other side. Thick or dense regions absorb more radiation. Thin regions, voids, and low density inclusions absorb less. The detector, whether film or a digital panel, receives more radiation wherever the part absorbed less, and that difference in transmitted intensity becomes a difference in darkness on the image. A gas pore inside a weld shows up as a dark spot. The image is a two dimensional shadow picture of everything in the beam path, which makes radiography a volumetric method. It examines the entire thickness, not just the surface, and it leaves a permanent record that can be reviewed years later. Visual, penetrant, and magnetic particle testing find surface and near surface conditions. Ultrasonics and radiography look inside the volume. Each method has strengths, and a good technician knows exactly where radiography fits. That power comes with a serious obligation. The same radiation that makes the image ionizes whatever it passes through, including you. Human senses give no warning at all. You cannot see, feel, smell, or hear a radiation field, and a dangerous exposure feels exactly like standing in an empty room. The early workers learned this the hard way. Thomas Edison's assistant died of radiation injuries in 1904. Young women who painted radium watch dials in the nineteen twenties suffered terrible bone disease. Those lessons were paid for in lives, and they are why radiation protection is built into everything a radiographer does. We control exposure with time, distance, and shielding. We carry instruments that measure dose because our bodies cannot. We survey after every single exposure, because the crank and the position indicator can lie, and the survey meter is the only witness you can trust. Typical regulations limit occupational whole body dose to five rem per year, and the working principle is to stay as far below every limit as reasonably achievable. Learn the physics in this course well. Every safety rule you will ever follow rests on it.
imagePrompt: A radiographer in flame-resistant coveralls kneels beside a large pipeline weld at dusk, connecting the drive cable of a gamma-ray projector while warning rope, radiation placards, and a survey meter mark the boundary behind him.

sections.heading: One Evening in 1895
sections.body:
On November 8, 1895, **Wilhelm Conrad Roentgen** was experimenting with a cathode-ray tube in Wurzburg, Germany. The tube was sealed inside black cardboard, yet a fluorescent screen across the room glowed every time the tube was energized. Something invisible was escaping the tube, penetrating the cardboard, and exciting the screen. Roentgen spent seven weeks characterizing the mystery rays: they traveled in straight lines, exposed photographic plates, passed easily through low-density materials, and were absorbed by dense ones like lead and bone. Not knowing their nature, he named them **X-rays** - X for unknown. His first radiograph, the bones of his wife's hand with her ring clearly visible, demonstrated the defining trick of our trade: making the inside of an object visible without cutting it open. The discovery earned Roentgen the first Nobel Prize in Physics in 1901.

For radiographers, two of Roentgen's observations still govern daily work: **penetration depends on density and thickness**, and **the rays expose photographic emulsion**. Those two facts are the entire basis of the radiographic image.

sections.heading: Becquerel, the Curies, and Natural Radioactivity
sections.body:
In 1896, **Henri Becquerel** stored wrapped photographic plates in a drawer with uranium salts and found the plates fogged - through opaque paper, with no tube, no electricity, and no sunlight. Uranium emitted penetrating radiation **spontaneously and continuously**. This was fundamentally different from Roentgen's machine-made rays: nothing had to be switched on, and nothing could be switched off.

**Marie and Pierre Curie** pursued the effect and by 1898 had discovered two previously unknown radioactive elements, **polonium** and **radium**. Marie Curie coined the word **radioactivity** for the spontaneous emission of radiation by unstable atoms.

These two discovery threads define the two source families used in industrial radiography to this day:

- **X-ray machines** - electrically generated radiation that stops the instant the power is removed.
- **Radioactive isotope (gamma) sources** - materials that emit radiation continuously and can never be turned off, only shielded.

Radium served as the first industrial gamma source in the 1930s. After World War II, nuclear reactors made artificial radioisotopes such as **iridium-192** and **cobalt-60**, which are safer to handle, far more compact, and remain the workhorses of field radiography.

sections.caption: Key milestones from Roentgen's discovery in 1895 to reactor-produced industrial gamma sources after World War II.

sections.heading: What Industrial Radiographic Testing Is
sections.body:
**Industrial radiographic testing (RT)** is a nondestructive testing method that passes penetrating ionizing radiation - X-rays or gamma rays - through a test object and records the transmitted radiation on a detector to form an image of the object's internal condition.

The working principle is **differential absorption**. As the beam crosses the part, the amount absorbed depends on the **thickness**, **density**, and **composition** of the material in each ray's path. A gas pore or shrinkage void is a region of missing metal: radiation crosses it with less absorption, so more reaches the detector behind it, producing a **darker** image area. A dense tungsten inclusion absorbs more than the surrounding steel and prints **lighter**. The result is a two-dimensional projection - a shadow picture - of everything in the beam path.

Because it examines the full thickness, RT is a **volumetric** method, and it produces a **permanent record** (film or digital file) that can be reviewed, audited, and compared years later. Typical applications include weld examination on pipelines and pressure vessels, castings, corrosion surveys, and verifying internal assembly of components. Its main limitations: access to both sides of the part is usually required, tightly closed planar flaws can be missed if poorly oriented to the beam, and the radiation hazard demands licensing, training, and discipline.

sections.caption: Differential absorption: an internal void absorbs less radiation than the surrounding steel, so more radiation reaches the film and the void images as a darker spot.
Where RT fits among the major NDT methods.

sections.title: You cannot sense radiation - your meter can
sections.body: Ionizing radiation is invisible, silent, odorless, and painless at the moment of exposure. A source stuck in the guide tube feels exactly like a source safely stored. **Never** approach an exposure device or guide tube without a functioning, calibrated **survey meter** in hand, and **survey after every exposure**. The meter - not the crank, not the position indicator - is the only trustworthy witness that the source is shielded.

sections.heading: Radiation Protection - Why It Comes First
sections.body:
Radiation makes its image by **ionization** - stripping electrons from atoms. The same process occurs in living tissue, where it can damage cells and DNA. High acute doses cause burns and radiation sickness; lower chronic doses raise long-term cancer risk. The early pioneers paid the tuition for this knowledge: Clarence Dally, Thomas Edison's X-ray assistant, died of radiation injuries in 1904, and the radium dial painters of the 1920s suffered devastating bone disease from ingested radium.

Modern industrial radiography is safe **only because** protection is engineered into every step. The framework you will use daily:

- **Time, distance, shielding** - the three levers of exposure control, applied under the **ALARA** principle (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).
- **Dose limits** - typical NRC and Agreement-State regulations limit occupational whole-body dose to **5 rem per year**, with operations planned to stay far below it.
- **Instrumentation** - radiographers typically wear a direct-reading dosimeter, a dosimetry badge, and an alarming ratemeter, and control a posted boundary (commonly at 2 mR/h).

Field radiography carries one of the higher routine dose potentials of any radiation occupation, and most serious incidents trace back to skipped surveys and assumed source positions. The Radiation Safety course covers this in depth; this course gives you the physics that makes every one of those rules make sense.

figures.title: Timeline: from discovery to industrial practice

checkQuestions.question: Why did Roentgen name his 1895 discovery 'X-rays'?
options: The rays were generated by an X-shaped electrode
He did not know the nature of the rays, so he used X for unknown
The rays crossed each other in an X pattern
X stood for the tenth ray type discovered that decade
checkQuestions.explanation: Roentgen could not identify what the penetrating rays were, so he borrowed the mathematician's symbol for an unknown quantity - X. The name stuck.

checkQuestions.question: What made Becquerel's 1896 discovery fundamentally different from Roentgen's X-rays?
options: His rays could not expose photographic plates
His rays came from an improved cathode-ray tube
The radiation was emitted spontaneously by uranium, with no tube or electrical power
His radiation could not penetrate paper wrapping
checkQuestions.explanation: Becquerel's uranium salts fogged wrapped plates with nothing energized - natural radioactivity is continuous and cannot be switched off, unlike machine-generated X-rays.

checkQuestions.question: A gas pore inside a weld appears as a dark spot on the radiograph because:
options: The void absorbs less radiation, so more radiation reaches the film behind it
The void absorbs more radiation than the surrounding steel
Gas inside the pore emits its own radiation
The film is thinner directly under the pore
checkQuestions.explanation: RT works by differential absorption: a void is missing metal, so radiation passes through that path with less attenuation, delivering more exposure and a darker image area on the film.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement best describes radiography's place among NDT methods?
options: A surface method, like penetrant testing, but faster
A volumetric method that produces a permanent image of internal conditions
A method limited to ferromagnetic materials
A method that requires access to only the source side in all techniques
checkQuestions.explanation: RT examines the full thickness of the part (volumetric) and records the result as a permanent film or digital image - a key advantage over methods that leave only a written report.

checkQuestions.question: Why is radiation protection considered fundamental to radiographic work, rather than an add-on?
options: Because radiation fields are visible only at night
Because human senses cannot detect ionizing radiation, so instruments and procedures are the only defense against tissue-damaging exposure
Because survey meters are too expensive to use on every shot
Because gamma sources can be switched off whenever a person approaches
checkQuestions.explanation: Ionizing radiation damages tissue yet gives no sensory warning at all. Surveys, dosimetry, and time-distance-shielding discipline are built into every task because the body provides no feedback.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic Radiographic Physics 1.1 (history and discovery)
CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic Radiographic Physics 1.2 (definition of industrial RT)
CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic Radiographic Physics 1.3 (radiation protection - why)
Codes such as ASME BPVC Section V, Article 2 (radiographic examination, general practice)
